slides.title: Heavy Data 🏋️
slides.content: Structs can become exceptionally massive, containing thousands of variables. Remember `Pass by Value`? If you pass a massive struct to a function, C has to photocopy the entire massive chunk of memory, which is incredibly slow.

slides.title: Pointer Power ⚡
slides.content:
Instead, we should pass **struct pointers** to functions. It's lightning fast because we just hand the function an 8-byte treasure map.

But there's a syntax trick you need to know.

slides.title: The Arrow Operator (->)
slides.content:
If you have a pointer to a struct, you CANNOT say `ptr.health = 100`.

You have to use the **Arrow Operator `->`**, which tells C: "Follow this pointer across memory, AND THEN grab the member."

quizData.question: When do you use the dot `.` operator versus the arrow `->` operator?

options.text: Dot is for ints, Arrow is for arrays.
Dot `.` is used on the real physical struct variable. Arrow `->` is used when you only have a Pointer pointing to a struct.
They are exactly the same; it's just stylistic preference.

quizData.explanation: If it's the real object, use `.`. If you are holding a remote control (a pointer) to the object, use `->` to bridge the gap.

quizData.question: Writing `(*ptr).health = 100` is the mathematically identical, harder-to-read version of writing `ptr->health = 100`.
quizData.explanation: Yes! The arrow operator was literally invented specifically because programmers got tired of typing `(*ptr).member`.
